Go back
A system of four objects for the living room. Not conceived individually but as parts of a whole — and the whole is called *ah ok* because that's what you say when something works without needing an explanation.

The brief

The starting point was the concept of Orografie: reinterpreting the fundamental amphibious concept in furniture design, with a reference to Mediterranean culture. The brief asked to move beyond traditional typological categorisations. To design new functions before new forms. The theoretical reference was twofold: on one side the morphological and typological analysis of Salvatore Greco, and on the other the need to recover the critical and radical capacity of Italian design as described by Vincenzo Castellana.

Five principles

Functions
Start from functions rather than forms to arrive at a new approach to needs.
Observe
Pay attention to the improper use people make of things in order to understand a need that can become a design seed.
Care
Dedicate care and attention to the objects around us, because in antiquity this meant respect for the context and for those who act within it.
Preserve
Design objects meant to be preserved and maintained rather than quickly replaced. Plan the lifespan of things so that they age healthily over time.
Quality
The quality of design forms, construction details, and raw materials is the best guarantee of permanence we have.

The concept

The process began with a deconstruction of the letters that make up "ah, ok". Each letter became the generative principle of an object: lines and circles as a fundamental modular unit, progressively transformed into functional forms through sketches and iterations. Form does not come first. Form follows function and function follows the observation of how people use spaces — often in ways that were never anticipated.

The objects

Chair a

A minimal seat whose defining characteristic is its ability to emphasise the expression "ah ok" depending on how many chairs one owns. The shape of the letter a becomes the load-bearing structure: an aluminium circle embracing the wooden seat.

Materials
Wood · Aluminium
Dimensions
586 × 421 × 572 mm

Lamp h

A floor lamp designed to be moved easily to any setting. It features a shelf intended for storing digital devices — the phone, the tablet, the things always kept within reach. The h is entirely in the aluminium arc that supports both the light and the shelf.

Materials
Wood · Aluminium · Glass
Dimensions
583 × 543 × 1165 mm

Coffee table o

A two-level coffee table that goes beyond its traditional function. The upper level is designed specifically to facilitate video calls and the use of digital devices. The circle of the o becomes the sliding base that allows the table to be oriented toward the user.

Materials
Wood · Aluminium · Glass
Dimensions
586 × 586 × 572 mm

Coat rack k

A functional coat rack that allows all accessories to be organised upon returning home — from keys to clothing. It includes a mirror for a last look before heading out. The k is the asymmetric structure that holds together the mirror, hooks, and accessory drawer.

Materials
Wood · Aluminium · Glass
Dimensions
586 × 586 × 1165 mm

The process

The initial moodboard sought to communicate lightness and fluidity — the concept of a domestic space that adapts to its inhabitants rather than the other way around. The selected images emphasised modularity, the versatility of postures, the possibility of reconfiguring a space without effort. From there the process moved through sketches, deconstruction of the letters, simplification of forms into lines and circles, and a series of iterations that led to the final forms. Each object went through many versions before arriving at the one published here. The system was also developed at a typographic level: an original typeface called *ahok Bold*, built on the same modular forms as the objects, adapts the project's geometries to the graphic plane.

The project was co-developed with Sofia Foster and Stefania Rapisarda.
